16.1.label: Question 16.1
16.1.synopsis: Economic capital: internal determination based on risk profile, risk appetite and on-going business strategy; calculated by projecting scenarios using stochastic modelling (for investment and mortality risks) or stress tests (where distribution less clearly understood); important issue is time horizon (one-year or full run-off). Regulatory capital: follows solvency requirements prescribed by regulators defining regulatory value of assets, liabilities and associated capital requirements; ladder of regulatory intervention between capital levels eg MCR and SCR under Solvency II. Rating agency capital: view of rating agencies on required capital; important for companies wishing to achieve or maintain a credit rating; rating agencies publish details of their approaches; calculated by replicating agencies' capital adequacy standards which increasingly embrace economic capital measures and ORSA under Solvency II
16.1.question: Define the three main approaches that are used by health and care insurance companies to decide how much capital they require and, for each approach, outline how the amount of required capital may be calculated.

Economic capital: This is an internal determination of the capital (ie assets in excess of liabilities) required.
It will reflect a company's risk profile, risk appetite and the needs of the company's on-going business strategy.
Economic capital may be calculated by projecting a number of scenarios. There are two approaches that are used to achieve this:
Stochastic modelling is commonly used to assess the investment and mortality risks, where probability distributions for the parameters concerned are available.
Stress tests are commonly used where the distribution of a particular risk is less clearly understood.

Regardless of the calculation approach, one important issue is the time horizon over which companies should calculate their economic capital requirements, eg one-year or full run-off of the in-force book.
Regulatory capital: This follows the solvency requirements prescribed by the regulators, which define the regulatory value of the company's assets, liabilities and the associated capital requirements.

Depending on the jurisdiction, there will likely be a ladder of regulatory intervention carried out between different capital requirement levels, for example between the MCR and SCR under Solvency II.
Rating agency capital: This is the view of the rating agencies on the amount of required capital.
It is important for companies who wish to achieve or maintain a particular credit rating.
The rating agencies make available details of the approaches they use to assess health and care insurance companies, which enables the companies to predict the likely rating agencies' view.

The amount of required capital under this measure could be calculated by trying to replicate these agencies' capital adequacy standards. In recent years, these capital adequacy standards have moved to embrace economic capital measures and other internal assessments of capital requirements, such as the ORSA under Solvency II.
